configs.label: Azure AKS
configs.description: Running Kubernetes on Azure Kubernetes Service. Covers AKS-specific controls for managed identity, IMDS access, and Azure Monitor.

configs.label: General Baseline
configs.description: Core security and operational items recommended for every Kubernetes cluster, regardless of provider or environment.

configs.label: CIS Kubernetes Benchmark v1.8
configs.description: Controls mapped to the Center for Internet Security Kubernetes Benchmark v1.8, covering API server hardening, etcd, kubelet, and workload configuration.

configs.label: Amazon EKS
configs.description: Running Kubernetes on Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service. Covers EKS-specific controls for IAM, metadata access, and AWS logging.

configs.label: Google GKE
configs.description: Running Kubernetes on Google Kubernetes Engine. Covers GKE-specific controls for Workload Identity, metadata server, and Cloud Audit Logs.

configs.label: High Availability
configs.description: Clusters configured for high availability with multiple control-plane nodes, where uptime and resilience are primary concerns.

configs.label: HIPAA
configs.description: Controls required to support HIPAA compliance for workloads that store, process, or transmit Protected Health Information (PHI).

configs.label: Multi-Cluster
configs.description: Operating more than one Kubernetes cluster, whether for availability, regional distribution, or environment separation.

configs.label: PCI-DSS v4.0
configs.description: Controls mapped to PCI-DSS v4.0 requirements for clusters that process, store, or transmit cardholder data or are in scope of the Cardholder Data Environment (CDE).

configs.label: Production Environment
configs.description: Additional controls required when operating a production-grade cluster with real user data and uptime expectations.

configs.label: Self-Managed / On-Prem
configs.description: Clusters running on bare metal, VMs, or private infrastructure where the operator is responsible for all control-plane security.

configs.label: SOC 2
configs.description: Controls supporting SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria (Security, Availability, Confidentiality) for Kubernetes workloads.

items.title: Place Worker Nodes on Private Subnets
items.description:
Worker nodes must reside exclusively on private subnets with no directly assigned public IP
addresses. Inbound traffic should reach workloads only through load balancers or ingress
controllers in the public subnet.

**What to check:** Verify that no worker node has a public IP. In AWS, check the node's
EC2 instance network interface. In GCP/Azure, verify no external IP is assigned.

**Remediation:** Reconfigure node groups/pools to use private subnets only. Route egress
traffic through a NAT gateway. Ensure load balancers handle all external-facing traffic.

references.title: AWS EKS Best Practices — Network Security
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Using Cloud Credentials

items.title: Restrict Network Access to API Server and Kubelet
items.description:
Limit network access to sensitive control-plane ports — API Server (6443) and Kubelet (10250)
— to authorised sources only, such as internal VPN ranges or bastion hosts. Exposing these
ports to the public internet is a critical risk.

**What to check:** Run a port scan or review security group / firewall rules. Neither port
should be reachable from 0.0.0.0/0.

**Remediation:** Restrict inbound rules to known CIDR ranges. Use a VPN or private link
service for operator access. Consider AWS PrivateLink / GCP Private Service Connect for
managed clusters.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Using Cloud Credentials
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Access Kubelet API

items.title: Apply Least-Privilege IAM Roles to Nodes
items.description:
IAM roles or service accounts attached to worker nodes must follow the principle of least
privilege. Nodes should only have permissions required to pull images, write logs, and
register with the control plane. Roles must not be shared across clusters.

**What to check:** Review the IAM role attached to each node group. Look for overly broad
policies (e.g., `*` actions or `*` resources). Verify no cross-cluster role sharing.

**Remediation:** Create dedicated, minimal IAM roles per cluster/node group. Use
permission boundaries. Prefer IRSA (AWS), Workload Identity (GCP), or AAD Pod Identity
(Azure) to grant per-pod permissions instead of node-level permissions.

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K08: Secrets Management Failures
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Access Cloud Resources
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Mount Service Principal

items.title: Block Pod Access to Cloud Metadata API
items.description:
The cloud instance metadata endpoint (e.g., 169.254.169.254 on AWS/GCP/Azure) exposes
node-level credentials and identity tokens. A compromised pod that can reach this endpoint
may escalate privileges to the underlying node or cloud account.

**What to check:** From inside a test pod, attempt to curl the metadata endpoint. On EKS,
verify IMDSv2 is enforced (hop limit = 1) so pods cannot reach it.

**Remediation:**
- **AWS EKS:** Set `httpPutResponseHopLimit: 1` on the node launch template to enforce IMDSv2.
- **All providers:** Create a NetworkPolicy or host firewall rule blocking egress to
  169.254.169.254 from pod CIDRs.

references.title: ISO 27001 A.8.8 — Management of Technical Vulnerabilities
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Instance Metadata API
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Mount Service Principal

items.title: Use a Container-Optimized Node OS
items.description:
General-purpose operating systems carry unnecessary tools, shells, and services that expand
the attack surface of every node. Container-optimized operating systems remove interactive
shells, lock the root filesystem read-only, and ship with a minimal package set.

**Recommended options:** Bottlerocket (AWS), Container-Optimized OS (GKE), RHCOS/CoreOS,
Flatcar Linux.

**What to check:** Inspect the AMI or node image used in the node group launch template.
Verify it is a purpose-built, hardened OS — not Ubuntu or Amazon Linux 2 general images.

**Remediation:** Migrate node groups to a container-optimised image. Test workload
compatibility before rolling out to production.

items.title: Disable Unnecessary Services and Ports on Node OS
items.description:
Every unused service or open port on a worker node is a potential entry point. Node
operating systems should be hardened by removing unnecessary packages and stopping or
masking all services not required for Kubernetes operation.

**What to check:** Run `ss -tlnp` or `netstat -tlnp` on a node. Identify any listening
ports that are not required by kubelet, container runtime, or CNI. Check `systemctl list-units`
for running services.

**Remediation:** Disable and mask unused systemd services. Remove unnecessary packages.
Apply OS-level firewall rules (iptables/nftables) to block non-essential ports. Automate
hardening with CIS Benchmark tooling (e.g., `cloud-init`, node configuration management).

items.title: Encrypt Node Storage at Rest
items.description:
All persistent volumes and node root disks must be encrypted at rest. Unencrypted disks
risk exposure of container data, etcd snapshots, and cached secrets if physical media is
recovered or cloud snapshots are shared inadvertently.

**What to check:** In AWS, verify EBS volumes are encrypted (EC2 → Volumes → Encryption).
In GCP, verify disk encryption is enabled. Check that the KMS key is customer-managed
(CMK) if compliance requires it.

**Remediation:** Enable default EBS/persistent disk encryption at the account or project
level. Use a Cloud KMS or HSM-backed key for regulated workloads. Rotate encryption keys
according to policy.

references.title: PCI-DSS Requirement 3.5 — Protect Stored Account Data

items.title: Maintain a Patch Management Process for Nodes
items.description:
Kubernetes nodes run a full OS stack. Unpatched kernels and system packages are a common
vector for container-escape and privilege-escalation attacks. Kubernetes minor versions
also have a support window of approximately 14 months — clusters must be upgraded before
end-of-support.

**What to check:** Compare the Kubernetes version of each node against the current release.
Check OS package patch dates. Verify a documented patch cadence exists (e.g., monthly OS
patching, quarterly K8s upgrades).

**Remediation:** Automate node pool rotation on a schedule. Use managed update channels
(EKS managed node groups, GKE release channels). Enable automated OS security patches via
the node OS tooling (e.g., `yum-cron`, `unattended-upgrades`).

references.title: SOC 2 CC7.4 — Incident Response and Recovery

items.title: Enable Immutable Infrastructure-Level Audit Logging
items.description:
Cloud-provider infrastructure logs (e.g., AWS CloudTrail, GCP Cloud Audit Logs, Azure
Activity Log) record API calls made to the cloud control plane — node creation, IAM
changes, storage access. These logs must be enabled and protected against tampering.

**What to check:** Verify CloudTrail / equivalent is enabled in all regions. Confirm logs
are delivered to an S3 bucket (or equivalent) with Object Lock (WORM) enabled and MFA
delete. Confirm log integrity validation is turned on.

**Remediation:** Enable management and data events. Route logs to a dedicated, centralised
account. Enable S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode. Set retention according to regulatory
requirements (HIPAA: 6 years, PCI-DSS: 1 year minimum).

items.title: Do Not Mount the Docker Socket into Containers
items.description:
Mounting the Docker daemon socket (`/var/run/docker.sock`) into a container grants that
container full control over the Docker daemon — effectively root on the host node. This is
a common container-escape vector exploited in CI/CD pipelines and build systems.

**What to check:** Search all workloads for volume mounts referencing `/var/run/docker.sock`.

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[].spec.volumes[]? | select(.hostPath.path == "/var/run/docker.sock")'
```

**Remediation:** Replace Docker-in-Docker approaches with rootless, socket-less alternatives
such as Kaniko, Buildah, or img for image builds. Use OPA/Kyverno admission policies to
block hostPath mounts of the Docker socket.

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K01: Insecure Workload Configurations

items.title: Use the Cluster Autoscaler for Highly Variable Workloads
items.description: For clusters experiencing highly variable workloads that occasionally outpace available node capacity, configure the Cluster Autoscaler. It manages creating new virtual machines/nodes (scale-up) when Pods can't be scheduled, and gracefully evicting empty nodes (scale-down) when resources are under-utilized.

items.title: Implement Log Retention and Archival
items.description:
Ensure a robust retention policy is set for your cluster and application logs. Maintain sufficient historical active logs 
(typically 30-45 days) for operational troubleshooting, and securely archive older logs to deep storage to meet compliance requirements.

items.title: Restrict Pod Access to Cloud Managed Identity
items.description:
Cloud providers assign managed identities to VM instances (EC2 Instance Profile on AWS,
Managed Identity on Azure, Service Account on GCP). Pods running on these VMs can inherit
the node's cloud identity and use it to access cloud services — including container
registries, secrets managers, and other Kubernetes clusters.

Attackers who compromise any pod can exploit this to escalate privileges to the node's
cloud identity, potentially gaining cloud-level control beyond the Kubernetes cluster.

**What to check:**
- Is IMDSv2 enforced with hop limit = 1 on AWS to prevent pod access to IMDS?
  ```
  aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[].Instances[].MetadataOptions'
  ```
- Are workloads using pod-level identity (IRSA, Workload Identity, AAD Workload Identity)
  instead of relying on node-level identity?

**Remediation:**
- **AWS:** Enable IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) so only specific pods get
  scoped cloud permissions. Set IMDS hop limit to 1.
- **GCP:** Use Workload Identity Federation.
- **Azure:** Use AAD Workload Identity.
- Do not assign broad IAM roles to node groups; grant pod-level permissions only.

references.title: AWS EKS — IAM Roles for Service Accounts
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Access Managed Identity Credentials

items.title: Restrict Cloud Account Permissions for Container Registry Access
items.description:
In cloud-managed Kubernetes (EKS, GKE, AKS), node IAM roles often include permissions
to pull images from the cloud's container registry (ECR, GCR, ACR). An attacker who
compromises any pod can abuse the node's cloud identity (via IMDS or Workload Identity)
to read images from private registries — potentially discovering proprietary code,
embedded secrets, or other sensitive assets.

**What to check:**
- Does the node IAM role include `AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryReadOnly` or equivalent?
  ```
  aws iam list-attached-role-policies --role-name <node-role>
  ```
- Is IMDS access from pods blocked (see CLD-004)?
- Are pod-level registry credentials scoped to only the registries each workload needs?

**Remediation:** Use pod-level identity (IRSA / Workload Identity) to grant registry pull
access only to specific service accounts that need it. Remove broad registry read policies
from node roles. Use separate private registries per team/environment with distinct access
controls to contain the blast radius of a compromised node.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Images from a Private Registry

items.title: Use a Cloud-Native Secret Manager for Sensitive Credentials
items.description:
Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded (not encrypted by default) and accessible to anyone
with RBAC read access. For high-value credentials — database passwords, API keys, TLS
private keys, third-party tokens — prefer cloud-native secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager,
GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault) which provide encryption, access auditing, automatic
rotation, and fine-grained IAM access control.

Integration with Kubernetes is achieved via the Secrets Store CSI Driver, which mounts
secrets from the cloud store directly into pod filesystems without creating Kubernetes
Secret objects.

**What to check:**
- Are high-value credentials stored as Kubernetes Secrets in plaintext?
  ```
  kubectl get secrets -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.type == "Opaque")'
  ```
- Is the Secrets Store CSI Driver installed?
  ```
  kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep secrets-store
  ```

**Remediation:** Migrate sensitive credentials to the cloud secret manager. Use the
Secrets Store CSI Driver with a `SecretProviderClass` to mount them as volumes.
Retain Kubernetes Secrets only for non-sensitive config values.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Application Credentials in Configuration Files

items.title: Enable the NodeRestriction Admission Controller
items.description:
The NodeRestriction admission controller limits the API objects that kubelets can modify.
Without it, a compromised kubelet (or a node-level attacker with kubelet credentials) can
modify any node or pod object in the cluster — potentially evicting workloads, reading
secrets from other nodes, or escalating privileges across the cluster.

With NodeRestriction enabled, a kubelet can only modify its own `Node` object and pods
scheduled to its own node. It cannot modify objects belonging to other nodes.

**What to check:**
```
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep enable-admission-plugins
```

`NodeRestriction` must appear in the `--enable-admission-plugins` list.

On managed clusters (EKS, GKE, AKS), this is enabled by default. Verify via the cloud
cluster configuration or CloudTrail/Audit logs.

**Remediation:** Add `NodeRestriction` to `--enable-admission-plugins` in the API server
manifest. Ensure kubelets authenticate with node-scoped credentials (bootstrap tokens or
signed certificates with system:node CN).

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Access Kubelet API

items.title: Disable Anonymous Authentication on the API Server
items.description:
By default, Kubernetes may allow unauthenticated requests to reach the API server. Setting
`--anonymous-auth=false` ensures every request must carry valid credentials, eliminating
a class of unauthenticated access attacks.

**What to check:** On the control-plane node, inspect the kube-apiserver static pod manifest
or process arguments:

```
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep anonymous-auth
```

The flag must be `--anonymous-auth=false`. If the flag is absent, the default is `true`
(insecure).

**Remediation:** Add `--anonymous-auth=false` to the kube-apiserver command arguments in
`/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`. On managed clusters (EKS/GKE/AKS), this
is typically controlled through cluster configuration options.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Access Kubernetes API Server
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Connect from Proxy Server

items.title: Set Authorization Mode to RBAC and Node
items.description:
The API server's `--authorization-mode` flag must be set to `RBAC,Node`. The `AlwaysAllow`
mode grants every request full access regardless of identity, which is never acceptable
in any environment.

**What to check:**

```
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep authorization-mode
```

The value must include `RBAC` and `Node`. It must not include `AlwaysAllow`.

**Remediation:** Set `--authorization-mode=Node,RBAC` in the kube-apiserver manifest.
Verify all existing RBAC policies are correct before removing AlwaysAllow if it was
previously in use.

references.title: PCI-DSS Requirement 7 — Restrict Access to System Components
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Access Kubernetes API Server

items.title: Ensure the API Server Secure Port is Not Disabled
items.description:
The `--secure-port` flag controls which port the API server listens on for HTTPS traffic.
Setting it to `0` disables TLS entirely, causing all API traffic to be unencrypted. This
must never be set to `0` in any environment.

**What to check:**

```
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep secure-port
```

If the flag is absent, the default (6443) is used, which is correct. A value of `0` is a
critical misconfiguration.

**Remediation:** Remove `--secure-port=0` if present, or set it to the desired port (default
6443). Ensure `--insecure-port=0` (the insecure port) is also disabled.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Access Kubernetes API Server

items.title: Enable TLS for API Server to Kubelet Communication
items.description:
The API server communicates with kubelets on every worker node. This channel must use TLS
to prevent eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks within the cluster network.

**What to check:**

```
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep kubelet-certificate-authority
```

The flags `--kubelet-certificate-authority`, `--kubelet-client-certificate`, and
`--kubelet-client-key` should all be set.

**Remediation:** Configure the API server with a kubelet client certificate signed by the
cluster CA. Ensure kubelets are configured with `--tls-cert-file` and `--tls-private-key-file`.

references.title: SOC 2 CC6.6 — Logical and Physical Access Controls

items.title: Enable Required Admission Controllers
items.description:
Admission controllers intercept API requests before objects are persisted, providing the
last line of defence against misconfigured workloads. The following must be enabled:
`NodeRestriction`, `PodSecurity`, and `LimitRanger`.

- **NodeRestriction:** Prevents kubelets from modifying objects outside their own node.
- **PodSecurity:** Enforces Pod Security Standards (baseline/restricted) at the namespace level.
- **LimitRanger:** Ensures pods cannot be created without resource limits.

**What to check:**

```
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep enable-admission-plugins
```

**Remediation:** Add the required plugins to `--enable-admission-plugins` in the API server
manifest. Verify existing workloads comply before enabling PodSecurity in Enforce mode.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Malicious Admission Controller

items.title: Encrypt Secrets at Rest in etcd
items.description:
Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded by default, which is not encryption. Anyone with
read access to etcd can trivially decode all secrets. Enabling `EncryptionConfiguration`
with a KMS or strong AES provider ensures secrets are encrypted before being written to disk.

**What to check:**

```
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep encryption-provider-config
```

The flag `--encryption-provider-config` must point to a valid EncryptionConfiguration file.
Verify the provider is `kms` (preferred) or `aescbc` — not `identity` (which is no-op).

**Remediation:** Create an EncryptionConfiguration manifest specifying the KMS provider
(AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault) or a generated AES-CBC key. Restart the API server
and run `kubectl get secret -o json | etcdctl get ...` to confirm encryption.

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Encrypting Secret Data at Rest
PCI-DSS Requirement 3 — Protect Stored Account Data
Microsoft Threat Matrix — List Kubernetes Secrets

items.title: Use a Dedicated Certificate Authority for etcd
items.description:
etcd is the backing store for all cluster state and secrets. Its certificate authority
must be separate from the main Kubernetes CA so that a compromised Kubernetes component
cannot forge etcd client certificates.

**What to check:**

```
ps aux | grep etcd | grep trusted-ca-file
```

Verify the CA file referenced by `--trusted-ca-file` is different from the Kubernetes
cluster CA (`/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt`).

**Remediation:** Generate a separate CA for etcd during cluster bootstrap (e.g., kubeadm
does this by default via `/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt`). Do not reuse the cluster CA
for etcd peer and client certificates.

items.title: Disable Anonymous Access to the Kubelet API
items.description:
The Kubelet exposes an HTTP API on port 10250 for the API server and debugging tools.
If anonymous access is enabled, unauthenticated users can execute commands in pods, read
logs, and retrieve sensitive runtime information.

**What to check:** On each node, inspect the kubelet configuration:

```
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml | grep -A2 anonymous
```

`authentication.anonymous.enabled` must be `false`.
`authorization.mode` must be `Webhook` (not `AlwaysAllow`).

**Remediation:** Set `authentication.anonymous.enabled: false` and
`authorization.mode: Webhook` in the KubeletConfiguration. Restart the kubelet.

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K09: Misconfigured Cluster Components

items.title: Audit and Remove Wildcard RBAC Permissions
items.description:
RBAC roles that use wildcard (`*`) verbs or resources grant far broader access than intended.
A single compromised service account with wildcard access can read all secrets, modify any
workload, or escalate privileges cluster-wide.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get clusterrolebindings,rolebindings -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[].rules[]? | select(.verbs[] == "*" or .resources[] == "*")'
```

**Remediation:** Replace wildcard rules with explicit, minimal permissions. Conduct a
quarterly review of all RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings. Remove any bindings that are
no longer required. Automate detection with tools such as RBAC Lookup, Krane, or Audit2RBAC.

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K03: Overly Permissive RBAC
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Exec into Container

items.title: Disable Automatic ServiceAccount Token Mounting
items.description:
By default, Kubernetes automatically mounts a ServiceAccount token into every pod. Most
application pods do not need to call the Kubernetes API, so this token is unnecessary and
becomes an attack surface — a compromised container can use it to probe the cluster.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.spec.automountServiceAccountToken != false) | .metadata.name'
```

Also check ServiceAccount objects:

```
kubectl get serviceaccounts -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.automountServiceAccountToken != false) | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Set `automountServiceAccountToken: false` on ServiceAccount objects and
override to `true` only in pods that explicitly require API access. Enforce this with an
admission policy (OPA Gatekeeper / Kyverno).

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K03: Overly Permissive RBAC
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Container Service Account

items.title: Enable API Server Audit Logging
items.description:
Kubernetes audit logs record every request to the API server — who made it, what resource
was accessed, and what the outcome was. Without audit logging, detecting breaches,
satisfying compliance audits, and investigating incidents is impossible.

**What to check:**

```
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep audit-policy-file
```

Flags `--audit-policy-file` and `--audit-log-path` must be set. The policy file must
capture at least `Metadata` level for all requests and `RequestResponse` for sensitive
resources (Secrets, ConfigMaps, ServiceAccounts).

**Remediation:** Create an audit policy YAML and pass it via `--audit-policy-file`. Configure
log rotation (`--audit-log-maxage`, `--audit-log-maxbackup`). Ship logs to a SIEM or
cloud-native logging service.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Delete Kubernetes Events

items.title: Apply Default-Deny NetworkPolicies in All Namespaces
items.description:
Without NetworkPolicies, every pod in a cluster can reach any other pod on any port.
A single compromised workload can freely probe databases, internal services, and the
metadata API. A default-deny policy in every namespace closes this attack path.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get networkpolicy -A
```

Every namespace must contain a default-deny-all ingress and egress policy. Verify with:

```
kubectl get networkpolicy -n <namespace> -o yaml | grep policyTypes
```

**Remediation:** Create a default-deny policy in every namespace, then selectively open
traffic with additional NetworkPolicies for legitimate flows. Use a CNI plugin that
enforces NetworkPolicies (Calico, Cilium, Weave Net — not flannel alone).

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Cluster Internal Networking
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Collecting Data from Pod
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Connect from Proxy Server

items.title: Do Not Use the Default Namespace for Workloads
items.description:
The `default` namespace has no resource quotas, no NetworkPolicies, and no PSS labels
applied by default. Deploying workloads into it bypasses namespace-level controls and
makes RBAC and network isolation harder to reason about.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get all -n default
```

Only system or test resources should ever be in the default namespace.

**Remediation:** Create dedicated namespaces per team, environment, or application.
Apply ResourceQuotas, LimitRanges, and NetworkPolicies to each namespace. Enforce this
with an admission policy that rejects workloads targeting the default namespace.

items.title: Run Multiple Replicas for Deployments
items.description: Never run a single Pod individually. A hardware failure of the physical machine, a kernel panic, or a node drain could cause your single Pod to be deleted and cause downtime. Instead, deploy your Pod as part of a Deployment, DaemonSet, ReplicaSet, or StatefulSet with more than one replica to guarantee availability.

items.title: Use Pod Anti-Affinity Rules to Spread Workload
items.description: Even if you run several replicas of your Pods, a node failure will take down all replicas if they are scheduled on the same node. Use Pod anti-affinity rules to enforce that Pods from the same Deployment are spread across different nodes in the cluster.

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Inter-Pod Affinity and Anti-Affinity
Kubernetes Docs — Assigning Pods to Nodes

items.title: Set Pod Disruption Budgets
items.description: When a node is drained, all Pods on that node are deleted and rescheduled, which could affect availability under heavy load. To protect Deployments from unexpected events that delete several Pods simultaneously, define a Pod Disruption Budget (e.g. guaranteeing at least N Pods are always running).

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Configure Pod Disruption Budgets

items.title: Define a LimitRange for Each Namespace
items.description: To prevent accidental deployment of Pods without explicit memory and CPU configurations, define a LimitRange object in each namespace. This enforce standard sizes or minimums/maximums for containers deployed in a namespace, acting as an automated safety net for resource utilization.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Denial of Service

items.title: Apply Technical Labels to Resources
items.description: Use technical labels to organize Kubernetes objects and assist in automation. Examples include grouping by application name (`app.kubernetes.io/name`), application instance (`app.kubernetes.io/instance`), version, architecture component, and management tool (`app.kubernetes.io/managed-by`). Apply them to all relevant workloads.

items.title: Apply Business Labels to Resources
items.description: To facilitate cost-allocation and tracking, map business logic into Kubernetes metadata. Common examples include identifying the assigned owner (`owner`), the project context (`project`), and the business unit (`business-unit`). Set these labels on your workloads to support external aggregation tools.

items.title: Apply Security Labels to Resources
items.description: Group resources by compliance demands and security requirements using labels. Key labels could represent the data-confidentiality level (`confidentiality`) or the compliance standard the workload must adhere to (`compliance: pci`). These metadata markers can simplify security assessments and auditing.

items.title: Avoid Sidecars for Logging
items.description: Instead of deploying a sidecar container in every Pod to capture or transform logs, applications should emit natively formatted logs directly to standard outputs. Avoiding unnecessary sidecars reduces cluster overhead and memory pressure. Prefer node-level logging daemons for aggregation.

items.title: Use Horizontal Pod Autoscaler for Variable Workloads
items.description: Rely on the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) to automatically add or remove Pod replicas based on current usage metrics (e.g. CPU, memory, custom metrics). This ensures the application remains responsive during traffic spikes without wasting resources during quiet periods.

items.title: Evaluate the Vertical Pod Autoscaler Carefully Before Using in Production
items.description:
The Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) automatically adjusts the CPU and memory requests and
limits of Pods based on observed usage. Unlike the HPA (which adds more replicas), the VPA
scales a single Pod's resources — useful for workloads that cannot scale horizontally.

**Key limitations to understand before adopting VPA in production:**
- Applying a resource change requires evicting and restarting the Pod (in-place resizing
  support via `InPlacePodVerticalScaling` is available as an alpha/beta feature gate —
  check your Kubernetes version).
- VPA and HPA must not target the same metric (e.g., both targeting CPU); only combine
  them using custom or external metrics for HPA.
- VPA recommendation mode (no automatic changes) is safe and useful for right-sizing.

**Recommended approach:** Run VPA in `Off` or `Initial` mode first to collect
recommendations. Validate the suggested values. Only switch to `Auto` mode after
confirming there are no disruption concerns for the workload.

items.title: Ensure Cluster Passes CIS Benchmark
items.description:
Check if your cluster complies with the Centre for Internet Security (CIS) benchmark for Kubernetes.
You can run tools like `kube-bench` to automate this process and report on misconfigurations. Note
that for managed clusters (like EKS, GKE, AKS), you won't be able to inspect the master nodes, but you
can still verify worker nodes.

items.title: Restrict Access to Alpha or Beta Features
items.description:
Alpha and beta Kubernetes features are in active development and may have limitations or bugs that result in security
vulnerabilities. Evaluate the trade-off of using these features. When in doubt, disable alpha/beta features you do not explicitly need.

items.title: Use OIDC for User Authentication
items.description:
Avoid static tokens or basic authentication for human users. Integrate OpenID Connect (OIDC) to allow single sign-on (SSO) 
from trusted identity providers (like Google Identity, AWS IAM, or traditional AD) to authenticate with the Kubernetes API.

references.title: The New Stack — Kubernetes Single Sign-On
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Using Cloud Credentials

items.title: Restrict ServiceAccount Tokens to Workloads
items.description:
ServiceAccount tokens are the preferred authentication strategy for applications and controllers running inside the cluster.
Do not distribute them or use them for human end-users trying to interact with the Kubernetes cluster API (use OIDC instead).

items.title: Collect Comprehensive Cluster Logs
items.description:
Gather logs across all functional levels of your cluster, including Nodes (kubelet, container runtime), Control Plane (API server,
scheduler, controller manager), and Kubernetes API auditing. Ensure log entries capture essential metadata like application name,
namespace, Pod name, container name, and node.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Delete Kubernetes Events
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Clear Container Logs

items.title: Provision a Log Aggregation Tool
items.description:
Use a log aggregation tool (like the EFK stack, DataDog, Sumo Logic, Stackdriver, or AWS CloudWatch) to gather and process logs. 
Logs scattered across ephemeral nodes will be lost when those nodes are naturally recycled; centralizing them is crucial for visibility and debugging.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Delete Kubernetes Events
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Clear Container Logs

items.title: Enforce ResourceQuotas for Namespaces
items.description:
While LimitRanges ensure individual Pods have sensible sizes, they don't stop someone from deploying 20,000 Pods or ConfigMaps.
Apply a ResourceQuota to every namespace to limit the total amount of CPU, memory, storage, and API objects (like Pods) that can be consumed across the entire namespace.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Denial of Service

items.title: Enforce Pod Security Standards via Pod Security Admission
items.description:
Kubernetes provides Pod Security Standards (PSS) — three predefined security profiles
(Privileged, Baseline, Restricted) — enforced at the namespace level by the built-in
Pod Security Admission (PSA) controller (available since Kubernetes 1.23, stable in 1.25).

> Note: PodSecurityPolicy (PSP) was removed in Kubernetes 1.25. If you are running an
> older cluster still using PSP, migrating to PSA or a policy engine (Kyverno, OPA
> Gatekeeper) is required before upgrading.

**Profiles:**
- `Privileged` — no restrictions (only for system/infrastructure namespaces)
- `Baseline` — prevents the most egregious misconfigurations (no privileged containers, no host namespaces)
- `Restricted` — follows current pod hardening best practices (non-root, read-only FS, seccomp)

**What to check:** Verify namespaces have a PSA label:
```
kubectl get namespaces -o json | jq '.items[].metadata.labels | select(."pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce" != null)'
```

**Remediation:** Label production namespaces with `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted`
(or `baseline` at minimum). Use `warn` and `audit` modes first to surface violations before
switching to `enforce`. Supplement with Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper for fine-grained policies.

items.title: Enforce Principle of Least Privilege in RBAC Policies
items.description:
Grant the least permissions needed for an entity to function. While broader grants can give unnecessary API access 
and might seem easier to manage initially, they pose a significant security risk. Start with an empty Role and 
add only the specific resources and verbs required for the ServiceAccount or User.

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K03: Overly Permissive RBAC
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Exec into Container

items.title: Create Granular, Non-Shared RBAC Roles
items.description:
Avoid sharing generic, broad policies across diverse workloads. Define custom, fine-grained roles based on distinct 
job functions or requirements (e.g., ReadOnly, PowerUser, Operator, Controller). This contains breaches by ensuring 
a compromised service account cannot affect unrelated namespaces or resource types.

items.title: Enable Network Policies via CNI Plugin
items.description:
By default, Kubernetes allows open communication between all Pods and nodes in the cluster. To enforce network 
segmentation, ensure you've installed a Container Network Interface (CNI) plugin (like Calico or Cilium) that 
actually supports Network Policies so your firewall rules can be honored.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Cluster Internal Networking

items.title: Restrict kubectl exec on Production Pods
items.description:
The `kubectl exec` command allows running arbitrary processes inside a running container.
In production, this capability should be restricted to break-glass scenarios only. An
attacker or compromised operator account with exec permissions can run malicious commands,
exfiltrate data, or install backdoors without deploying new workloads.

**What to check:** Audit which RBAC users and service accounts have `pods/exec` permissions:

```
kubectl auth can-i create pods/exec --all-namespaces --as <user-or-sa>
kubectl get clusterrolebindings,rolebindings -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.rules[]?.resources[]? == "pods/exec") | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Remove `pods/exec` from all non-operator roles. If exec is needed for
debugging, grant it via a time-limited RoleBinding (e.g., via JIT access tooling) rather
than a permanent grant. Use an admission controller (OPA Gatekeeper / Kyverno) to restrict
exec to specific namespaces or labels.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Exec into Container

items.title: Audit and Restrict CronJob Creation Permissions
items.description:
Kubernetes CronJobs can be used by attackers with sufficient RBAC permissions to schedule
persistent execution of malicious code inside the cluster. Unlike a standalone pod,
a CronJob survives restarts and continues executing on a schedule, making it an ideal
persistence mechanism.

**What to check:** Identify which accounts can create CronJobs or Jobs:

```
kubectl auth can-i create cronjobs --all-namespaces --as <user-or-sa>
kubectl get clusterrolebindings,rolebindings -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.rules[]?.resources[]? | test("cronjobs|jobs")) | .metadata.name'
```

Also review existing CronJobs for suspicious or unexpected entries:
```
kubectl get cronjobs -A
```

**Remediation:** Restrict `create`, `patch`, and `update` permissions on `cronjobs`
resources to only CI/CD service accounts that legitimately manage them. Alert on
unexpected CronJob creation via audit log analysis.

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K03: Overly Permissive RBAC

items.title: Protect Static Pod Manifests Directory
items.description:
Static pods are managed directly by kubelet on each node by reading YAML manifests from a
local directory (typically `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/`). Attackers who gain write access
to this directory can create or modify pods that run outside the Kubernetes API server's
control — bypassing admission controllers, RBAC, and audit logging.

Kubelet also supports a `--manifest-url` flag that loads manifests from a remote URL.
Both vectors must be locked down.

**What to check:**
- Verify `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/` is only writable by root:
  ```
  stat -c "%a %U" /etc/kubernetes/manifests
  ```
- Verify kubelet is not started with `--manifest-url`:
  ```
  ps aux | grep kubelet | grep manifest-url
  ```

**Remediation:** Set directory permissions to `700` owned by `root`. Disable `--manifest-url`
on all kubelets if not in use. Monitor the directory for unexpected file changes using a
file integrity monitoring (FIM) tool.

items.title: Detect and Alert on Kubernetes Event Deletion
items.description:
Kubernetes events record state changes and failures across cluster resources (pod scheduling,
image pulls, OOMKills, etc.). Attackers who have gained cluster access may delete these
events to erase evidence of their activity — for example via `kubectl delete events --all`.

API server audit logs record event deletion and must be routed to tamper-resistant external
storage so the audit trail cannot be erased alongside the events themselves.

**What to check:** Verify audit logging is enabled and captures event delete actions:
```
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep audit-policy-file
```

Confirm the audit policy captures `delete` on `events` at `RequestResponse` level.
Verify logs are shipped to external storage (not only local disk).

**Remediation:** Update the audit policy to capture `delete` on resource `events` in
the `""` and `events.k8s.io` API groups. Ship audit logs to an immutable sink (S3,
CloudWatch, Splunk). Alert on `kubectl delete events` patterns in the SIEM.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Delete Kubernetes Events

items.title: Restrict CoreDNS Configuration Write Access
items.description:
CoreDNS is the cluster's built-in DNS server. Its configuration is stored in the `coredns`
ConfigMap in the `kube-system` namespace. An attacker with write access to this ConfigMap
can inject malicious DNS rewrites, redirecting cluster-internal traffic to adversary-controlled
endpoints — a technique known as CoreDNS poisoning.

**What to check:** Verify who can patch or update the CoreDNS ConfigMap:
```
kubectl auth can-i update configmap/coredns -n kube-system --as <user-or-sa>
```

Review all RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings that grant `update`/`patch`/`create`
on ConfigMaps in `kube-system`.

**Remediation:** Remove ConfigMap write permissions from non-infrastructure service accounts.
Use a Kyverno or OPA policy to protect the `coredns` ConfigMap specifically. Monitor the
ConfigMap for unexpected changes via audit logging or GitOps drift detection.

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — DNS for Services and Pods

items.title: Use a CNI Plugin That Prevents ARP Spoofing
items.description:
Kubernetes' default networking (kubenet) relies on ARP for pod-to-pod communication and is
susceptible to ARP poisoning/IP spoofing attacks. A compromised pod can broadcast fake ARP
replies to intercept or impersonate traffic from other pods on the same node.

CNI plugins that use eBPF or enforce strict ARP behaviour (e.g., Cilium, Calico with eBPF
dataplane) eliminate this attack vector at the network level.

**What to check:** Identify which CNI plugin is installed:
```
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o wide | grep -E 'calico|cilium|flannel|weave'
ls /etc/cni/net.d/
```

Flannel alone does **not** prevent ARP poisoning. Calico in standard mode uses BGP routing
and is not susceptible; Cilium in eBPF mode fully prevents it.

**Remediation:** If using a CNI prone to ARP poisoning (e.g., flannel), plan a migration
to Cilium or Calico. If staying on Calico, enable WireGuard encryption for in-cluster
traffic to prevent interception even if ARP is spoofed.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — ARP Poisoning and IP Spoofing

items.title: Restrict Access to Kubernetes Dashboard and Sensitive Interfaces
items.description:
Management UIs such as the Kubernetes Dashboard, Argo Workflows, Kubeflow, and Weave Scope
provide broad cluster visibility and control. If exposed to the internet or accessible inside
the cluster without strong authentication, they become high-value attack targets. Attackers
who gain access can enumerate resources, create workloads, and exfiltrate data using the
dashboard's service account permissions.

**What to check:**
- Is the Kubernetes Dashboard reachable from the internet?
  ```
  kubectl get svc -n kubernetes-dashboard
  ```
- Does the dashboard's service account have overly broad ClusterRole bindings?
  ```
  kubectl get clusterrolebinding kubernetes-dashboard -o yaml 2>/dev/null
  ```
- Are management interfaces accessible within the cluster without authentication?

**Remediation:** Never expose management UIs via a public LoadBalancer or NodePort.
Require authentication (OIDC, SSO) for all such interfaces. Apply least-privilege RBAC
to dashboard service accounts. Prefer `kubectl port-forward` for operator access.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Exposed Sensitive Interfaces

items.title: Restrict Creation of ClusterRoleBindings to cluster-admin
items.description:
The built-in `cluster-admin` ClusterRole grants unrestricted access to every resource and
operation in the cluster. An attacker or compromised account with permission to create
`ClusterRoleBindings` can escalate their own privileges by binding any identity to
`cluster-admin`, effectively taking over the cluster.

**What to check:** Identify who can create or modify ClusterRoleBindings:
```
kubectl auth can-i create clusterrolebindings --all-namespaces --as <user-or-sa>
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.roleRef.name == "cluster-admin") | .subjects'
```

**Remediation:** Restrict `create`, `update`, `patch`, and `delete` on `clusterrolebindings`
to a very small set of break-glass admin accounts only. Regularly audit and trim the list
of cluster-admin bindings. Alert via audit logs whenever a new ClusterRoleBinding is created
targeting `cluster-admin`.

items.title: Restrict Kubeconfig File Permissions and Distribution
items.description:
The kubeconfig file contains cluster endpoint URLs and credentials (certificates, tokens)
that grant API access. If an attacker obtains a kubeconfig file from a compromised developer
machine, CI/CD system, or cloud storage, they can gain access to the cluster from outside
the network perimeter without any additional authentication.

**What to check:**
- Is the kubeconfig file stored on shared or poorly secured systems?
- Are kubeconfig files committed to version control?
  ```
  git log --all --full-history -- "**kubeconfig*" "**kube/config*"
  ```
- Are CI/CD secrets containing kubeconfig rotated regularly?
- Do kubeconfig files contain cluster-admin level credentials?

**Remediation:** Store kubeconfig files only on trusted operator workstations with OS-level
encryption. Use short-lived credentials (OIDC tokens, `aws eks get-token`) rather than
long-lived certs. Rotate kubeconfig credentials on a schedule. Scan CI/CD secrets for
stale or over-privileged kubeconfig entries.

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Organizing Cluster Access Using kubeconfig

items.title: Audit Secret Access and Remove Unused Secrets
items.description:
Kubernetes Secrets store sensitive data such as passwords, API keys, and TLS certificates.
Attackers with access to a pod's service account or direct etcd access can list and read
Secrets, exposing credentials that enable lateral movement and privilege escalation.

**What to check:**
- Audit who can list or get Secrets cluster-wide:
  ```
  kubectl auth can-i list secrets --all-namespaces --as <user-or-sa>
  kubectl get clusterrolebindings,rolebindings -A -o json | \
    jq '.items[] | select(.rules[]?.resources[]? == "secrets") | .metadata.name'
  ```
- List all Secrets and identify unused ones:
  ```
  kubectl get secrets -A
  ```

**Remediation:** Restrict `list` and `get` on `secrets` to only the service accounts
that require them. Remove all orphaned secrets (not mounted by any pod or volume).
Prefer cloud-native secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key
Vault) over Kubernetes Secrets for high-value credentials. Rotate leaked secrets immediately.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — List Kubernetes Secrets

items.title: Enforce Pod Naming Conventions to Detect Masquerading
items.description:
Attackers may name backdoor pods to resemble legitimate system pods — for example, naming
a malicious pod `coredns-a1b2c3` to blend in with CoreDNS pods, or deploying to `kube-system`
to appear as infrastructure. This masquerading technique makes detection harder in manual
inspection and noisy dashboards.

**What to check:**
- Are all pods in `kube-system` and other system namespaces created by known controllers?
  ```
  kubectl get pods -n kube-system -o json | \
    jq '.items[] | select(.metadata.ownerReferences == null) | .metadata.name'
  ```
- Do any pods have suspicious names mimicking system components in non-system namespaces?

**Remediation:** Use admission policies (Kyverno/OPA) to enforce naming conventions and
label requirements for all pods. Restrict pod creation in `kube-system` to cluster
infrastructure service accounts only. Alert on orphaned pods (no ownerReference) in system
namespaces. Use GitOps drift detection to catch unmanaged resources.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Pod or Container Name Similarity

items.title: Implement Data Backup Strategy for Persistent Volumes
items.description:
Attackers with sufficient permissions may destroy persistent data by deleting PersistentVolumes,
PersistentVolumeClaims, or the underlying cloud storage. Without a tested backup strategy,
data destruction results in permanent data loss and severe availability incidents.

**What to check:**
- Are backups taken for each PersistentVolumeClaim used by stateful workloads?
- Are backups stored in a separate account, region, or storage tier from the cluster?
- Is the restore path tested regularly?
  ```
  kubectl get pvc -A
  kubectl get volumesnapshots -A 2>/dev/null
  ```

**Remediation:** Use cloud-native volume snapshot APIs (AWS EBS Snapshots, GCP Persistent
Disk Snapshots, Azure Disk Snapshots) scheduled via Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot objects or
tools like Velero. Store snapshots in a separate, restricted account. Test restore procedures
at least quarterly. Apply RBAC to restrict who can delete PVCs and PVs.

references.title: Velero — Backup and Restore for Kubernetes

items.title: Enforce Pod Security Standards at Namespace Level
items.description:
Pod Security Standards (PSS) define three profiles — Privileged, Baseline, and Restricted —
that control which security-sensitive fields pods may set. Namespaces must be labelled to
enforce at least the Baseline profile; production namespaces should target Restricted.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get namespaces -o json | \
  jq '.items[].metadata.labels | with_entries(select(.key | startswith("pod-security.kubernetes.io")))'
```

Every non-system namespace should have `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce` set to
`baseline` or `restricted`.

**Remediation:** Add PSS labels to all namespaces. Use `warn` and `audit` modes first to
detect violations before switching to `enforce`. Consider OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno for
more fine-grained policies.

items.title: Prohibit Privileged Containers
items.description:
A container running with `privileged: true` has unrestricted access to the host kernel and
devices. It can load kernel modules, modify host networking, and escape the container
boundary entirely. No production workload should require privileged mode.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged == true) | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Remove `privileged: true` from all container security contexts. If a
workload legitimately needs elevated capabilities, grant only the specific Linux capabilities
required via `capabilities.add` rather than full privileged mode. Block this with a PSS
Baseline/Restricted label or a Kyverno policy.

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K01: Insecure Workload Configurations

items.title: Run Containers as Non-Root
items.description:
Containers running as UID 0 (root) can exploit vulnerabilities to gain root access on the
host node. Setting `runAsNonRoot: true` ensures the container's process runs as an
unprivileged user, limiting blast radius if the container is compromised.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(
    (.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot != true) and
    (.spec.containers[].securityContext.runAsNonRoot != true)
  ) | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Add `securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true` and `securityContext.runAsUser: <non-zero UID>`
to pod and container specs. Update base images to create and use a non-root user via
`USER <uid>` in the Dockerfile.

items.title: Disable Privilege Escalation
items.description:
The `allowPrivilegeEscalation` flag controls whether a process can gain more privileges
than its parent. Even when a container starts as non-root, a vulnerability in the binary
could trigger a setuid/setgid elevation if this flag is not explicitly disabled.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(
    .spec.containers[].securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation != false
  ) | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Explicitly set `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` in every container's
`securityContext`. This is required by the PSS Restricted profile and can be enforced
with a Kyverno ClusterPolicy or OPA constraint.

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K01: Insecure Workload Configurations

items.title: Drop All Linux Capabilities
items.description:
Linux capabilities are fine-grained subsets of root privileges. Containers inherit a
default set that includes dangerous capabilities like `NET_RAW`, `SYS_CHROOT`, and others.
Dropping all capabilities and adding back only what is strictly needed reduces the blast
radius of a container compromise.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(
    .spec.containers[].securityContext.capabilities.drop | index("ALL") | not
  ) | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Add the following to every container's `securityContext`:

```yaml
capabilities:
  drop: ["ALL"]
  add: []   # only add specific caps if truly required, e.g. NET_BIND_SERVICE
```

items.title: Apply a Seccomp Profile to All Containers
items.description:
Seccomp (Secure Computing Mode) filters the system calls a container can make to the
Linux kernel. Without a seccomp profile, a compromised container can invoke dangerous
syscalls (e.g., `ptrace`, `mount`, `keyctl`) that may lead to container escape.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(
    .spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type == null and
    .spec.containers[].securityContext.seccompProfile.type == null
  ) | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Set `seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault` (or `Localhost` with a custom
profile) at the pod `securityContext` level. The RuntimeDefault profile is safe for
virtually all workloads and is required by PSS Restricted.

```yaml
securityContext:
  seccompProfile:
    type: RuntimeDefault
```

items.title: Use a Read-Only Root Filesystem
items.description:
A writable root filesystem allows an attacker who gains code execution inside a container
to write malicious scripts, modify binaries, or persist backdoors across restarts. Setting
`readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` closes this vector.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(
    .spec.containers[].securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem != true
  ) | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Add `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` to every container's `securityContext`.
Mount `emptyDir` volumes for paths that legitimately need write access (e.g., `/tmp`,
application cache directories). Update Dockerfiles to not write to the image root at runtime.

references.title: PCI-DSS Requirement 5 — Protect All Systems Against Malware
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Writable hostPath Mount

items.title: Set Memory Limits and Resource Requests on All Containers
items.description:
> **Memory limits** are strongly recommended for all containers.
> **CPU limits** require careful consideration — see CNT-017 before enabling them.

Containers without resource limits can consume unbounded CPU and memory, causing resource
starvation for co-located workloads (noisy-neighbour problem) or enabling denial-of-service
through resource exhaustion. This directly violates the Availability criteria in SOC 2.

**What to check:**

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(
    .spec.containers[] | (.resources.limits.cpu == null or .resources.limits.memory == null)
  ) | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Define `resources.requests` and `resources.limits` for CPU and memory on
every container. Use `LimitRange` objects to set namespace-wide defaults and maxima.
Use VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler) in recommendation mode to size limits accurately.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Denial of Service

items.title: Deploy Runtime Threat Detection
items.description:
Static controls (PSS, RBAC, NetworkPolicies) cannot detect threats that occur after a
container has started. Runtime security tools such as Falco (CNCF), Tetragon, or AWS
GuardDuty for EKS monitor system calls and network activity in real time to detect
anomalous behaviour — such as a shell being opened inside a production container or
sensitive files being read.

**What to check:** Verify a runtime security agent is deployed as a DaemonSet on all nodes:

```
kubectl get daemonset -A | grep -E 'falco|tetragon|guardduty'
```

Confirm alerting rules are active and integrated with a SIEM or alerting pipeline.

**Remediation:** Deploy Falco via Helm. Configure rules to alert on: shells in containers,
unexpected outbound connections, writes to sensitive paths, and privilege changes. Wire
alerts to PagerDuty, Slack, or a SIEM.

references.title: PCI-DSS Requirement 11.5 — Detect and Respond to Threats
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Bash or Cmd Inside Container

items.title: Configure Readiness Probes for All Containers
items.description:
Kubernetes routes traffic to a pod only when its readiness probe succeeds. Without a
readiness probe, the kubelet assumes the app is ready the moment the container starts —
which means requests may be sent to a pod that hasn't finished initialising, causing errors.

**Key rules:**
- The readiness probe must not check downstream dependencies (databases, APIs). Only check
  local state. A failing dependency should not make your pod unreachable — that spreads
  the failure upstream. See also CNT-013.
- Use a separate endpoint from the liveness probe (e.g., `/readyz`).
- Set `initialDelaySeconds` and `failureThreshold` appropriate to your app's startup time.

**What to check:**
```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[].readinessProbe == null) | .metadata.name'
```

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes

items.title: Let the Container Crash on Fatal Errors
items.description:
When an application reaches an unrecoverable error — an uncaught exception, a missing
dependency, or a corrupted configuration — it should exit immediately and let the kubelet
restart the container. Do not suppress fatal errors, swallow panics, or use the liveness
probe to signal fatal conditions.

Kubernetes is designed around the assumption that containers are ephemeral and
restartable. An application that catches fatal errors and keeps running causes several
problems:
- The pod appears healthy but is not serving traffic correctly.
- Debug logs and crash information are lost.
- The liveness probe may aggressively restart the container instead of the process
  cleanly exiting, leading to connection disruption.

**What to check:**
- Does the application exit with a non-zero status code on unrecoverable errors?
- Is `restartPolicy: Always` set (this is the default)?
- Are liveness probe handlers *not* checking application logic conditions?

**Remediation:** In your framework, let unhandled exceptions propagate to the top-level
process and exit with a non-zero code. Do not write liveness probe handlers that fail on
application logic errors (e.g., "cannot connect to DB"). Reserve liveness for deadlock
detection only (see CNT-012).

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes

items.title: Configure a Passive Liveness Probe
items.description:
The liveness probe is a deadlock-detection mechanism — not a health check for application
logic. Its sole purpose is to detect a process that is still running but no longer
responsive (e.g., stuck in an infinite loop, deadlocked threads). The liveness probe
endpoint must always return success as long as the process can respond at all.

**Rules for a correct liveness probe:**
- Use a dedicated, lightweight endpoint (e.g., `/livez`) that returns HTTP 200 unconditionally.
- Never include downstream dependency checks (database, cache, APIs) in this endpoint.
- Never use the same endpoint as the readiness probe — see CNT-013.
- Set a generous `initialDelaySeconds` and `failureThreshold` to avoid premature restarts
  during slow application startups.

**What to check:**
```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.spec.containers[].livenessProbe == null) | .metadata.name'
```

**Remediation:** Add a `/livez` route to your application server that returns `200 OK`
without performing any work. Configure `livenessProbe.httpGet.path: /livez` in the pod
spec with appropriate thresholds for your workload's startup time.

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes

items.title: Use Separate Endpoints for Liveness and Readiness Probes
items.description:
When liveness and readiness probes point to the same endpoint, the kubelet simultaneously
removes the pod from the Service load balancer *and* restarts it. This combines the
effects of both probes and leaves no window for connections to drain, causing dropped
requests.

- **Readiness probe** (`/readyz`): checks if the app can serve traffic right now.
  May check local warm-up state or required configuration. Must not check downstream
  services (see CNT-010).
- **Liveness probe** (`/livez`): checks if the process is still responsive.
  Must be a trivial endpoint that always succeeds unless the process is deadlocked.

**What to check:** Identify pods where both probes share the same path:
```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[].spec.containers[] | select(
    .livenessProbe.httpGet.path == .readinessProbe.httpGet.path
  ) | .name'
```

**Remediation:** Expose two distinct endpoints (`/livez` and `/readyz`). Configure each
probe independently. The liveness endpoint must be simpler and lighter-weight than the
readiness endpoint.

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes
Handling Client Requests Properly with Kubernetes

items.title: Gracefully Drain Connections on SIGTERM
items.description:
When Kubernetes terminates a pod, it sends SIGTERM to the container and waits for
`terminationGracePeriodSeconds` (default: 30s) before sending SIGKILL. During this window,
traffic may still arrive because endpoint propagation to kube-proxy and Ingress controllers
is asynchronous — the pod address may still be in active use.

The application must handle SIGTERM by:
1. Stopping acceptance of new incoming connections.
2. Completing all active in-flight requests.
3. Closing idle keep-alive connections.
4. Exiting cleanly.

**What to check:**
- Does the app implement a SIGTERM handler?
- Is `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` set long enough to drain requests for your workload?
- Is a `preStop` sleep hook used to allow endpoint propagation before shutdown begins?

**Remediation:** Implement a SIGTERM handler in the application. Consider adding a
`preStop: exec: command: ["sleep", "5"]` lifecycle hook to give kube-proxy time to remove
the pod from endpoints before the app starts draining. Adjust `terminationGracePeriodSeconds`
to match the maximum expected request duration.

```yaml
lifecycle:
  preStop:
    exec:
      command: ["sleep", "5"]
```

references.title: Handling Client Requests Properly with Kubernetes

items.title: Forward SIGTERM to the Application Process in Dockerfile
items.description:
When a container's `CMD` or `ENTRYPOINT` uses **shell form** rather than **exec form**,
the shell (`/bin/sh`) becomes PID 1. Shells do not forward signals to child processes by
default. As a result, SIGTERM sent by Kubernetes is silently swallowed, and the app waits
the full `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` before being force-killed — causing connection
drops and slow rollouts.

**Shell form (incorrect):**
```dockerfile
CMD node server.js          # PID 1 = /bin/sh -c, does not forward SIGTERM
ENTRYPOINT ./start.sh       # same issue
```

**Exec form (correct):**
```dockerfile
CMD ["node", "server.js"]   # PID 1 = node, receives SIGTERM directly
ENTRYPOINT ["/app/start"]
```

**If a wrapper script is required,** end the script with `exec "$@"` so the shell is
replaced by the target process:
```bash
#!/bin/sh
# ... pre-start setup ...
exec "$@"
```

**What to check:** Review all Dockerfiles in your repositories. Verify that the final
`CMD` and `ENTRYPOINT` instructions use JSON array (exec) form.

items.title: Keep CPU Requests to 1 CPU or Below
items.description: Unless running computational intensive jobs, it is recommended to set your CPU requests to 1 CPU or below. Excessive CPU requests constrain the scheduler and may prevent Pods from being optimally binned and packed on available nodes.

references.title: Understanding Resource Limits in Kubernetes: CPU Time

items.title: Avoid CPU Limits Unless Necessary
items.description: Because CPU is a compressible resource, hitting a CPU limit will cause the process to be throttled, possibly prematurely. In the case of multithreaded applications, throttling can degrade performance unexpectedly. Consider relying on CPU requests instead, and keep CPU limits removed unless you have a specific and well-justified use case.

references.title: Understanding Resource Limits in Kubernetes: CPU Time
CPU Limits and Throttling in Kubernetes

items.title: Configure the Appropriate Quality of Service (QoS)
items.description: When a node goes into an overcommitted state, Kubernetes evicts Pods based on their QoS class (Guaranteed, Burstable, BestEffort). By correctly configuring CPU/Memory requests and limits, you dictate the QoS for your Pods and determine their eviction priority.

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Configure Quality of Service for Pods

items.title: Use Ephemeral Local Filesystems
items.description: Containers should not store persistent state in their local filesystem. Local state prevents horizontal scaling and causes discrepancies between Pod replicas over time. Use an external storage service or a PersistentVolume mounted in the Pod to ensure state persists reliably and independent of the container lifecycle.

items.title: Mount Secrets as Volumes Rather Than Environment Variables
items.description: Avoid storing sensitive configurations (credentials, tokens, certificates) as environment variables in containers. Environment variables can sometimes be inspected via system tools, crash dumps, or error logs. Always mount Secret objects securely as temporary tmpfs volumes.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Application Credentials in Configuration Files

items.title: Do Not Run SSH Daemon Inside Containers
items.description:
Running an SSH server inside a container violates the principle of single-process containers
and creates an independent authentication surface. Attackers who obtain valid SSH credentials
— through brute force, leaked keys, or phishing — can use this to gain persistent remote
access to the container, bypassing Kubernetes RBAC entirely.

**What to check:** Scan running containers for listening SSH daemons:
```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name)"' | \
  xargs -I {} kubectl exec -n $(echo {} | cut -d/ -f1) $(echo {} | cut -d/ -f2) \
  -- ss -tlnp 2>/dev/null | grep ':22'
```

Also scan container images in the pipeline:
```
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL <image> | grep -i ssh
```

**Remediation:** Remove SSH daemon packages from all container images. Use `kubectl exec`
(restricted via RBAC — see CLT-035) for legitimate operator access. Never expose port 22
from any container. Use distroless or minimal base images that do not include SSH by default.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — SSH Server Running Inside Container
OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K01: Insecure Workload Configurations

items.title: Restrict hostPath Volume Mounts
items.description:
A `hostPath` volume mounts a file or directory from the host node's filesystem into the
container. This creates a direct bridge between the container and the host — allowing
attackers who compromise the container to read sensitive host files, write to host paths
(including cron directories and init scripts), or escape the container boundary entirely.

Writable hostPath mounts are one of the most common container escape vectors.

**What to check:** Find all pods using hostPath volumes:
```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.spec.volumes[]?.hostPath != null) |
    {name: .metadata.name, ns: .metadata.namespace,
     paths: [.spec.volumes[].hostPath.path? | select(.)]}'
```

**Remediation:**
- Block hostPath mounts via Pod Security Standards (`restricted` profile disallows them).
- Use Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper to create an allow-list of approved hostPath paths (e.g., `/var/log`).
- Any permitted hostPath must be mounted `readOnly: true`.
- Prefer `emptyDir`, ConfigMap, or PVC volumes for container data needs.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Writable hostPath Mount

items.title: Detect and Block Backdoor Container Images
items.description:
A backdoor container is a legitimate-looking container image that has been tampered with
to include malicious code — such as a reverse shell, cryptominer, or data exfiltration
agent. This can happen through compromised base images, CI/CD supply chain attacks, or
registry hijacking.

**What to check:**
1. Are all images signed and verified at admission? (see CODE-005)
2. Does the CI pipeline scan images for malware and unexpected processes?
   ```
   trivy image --scanners vuln,secret,misconfig <image>
   ```
3. Are runtime processes monitored for anomalous behaviour?
   ```
   kubectl get daemonset -n falco  # or equivalent runtime detection
   ```

**Remediation:** Enforce image signing with Cosign + a Kyverno/Connaisseur admission
policy that rejects unsigned images. Scan all images in CI before push and at admission.
Deploy Falco rules that alert on unexpected outbound connections or new processes in
containers. Use distroless or minimal base images to reduce the blast radius.

items.title: Block Sidecar Injection from Untrusted Sources
items.description:
Sidecar containers share the network namespace and pod lifecycle of the main container,
making them a powerful injection point. Attackers with permissions to modify pod definitions
(e.g., via a compromised service account or mutating admission webhook) can inject malicious
sidecars into legitimate pods to intercept traffic, exfiltrate data, or maintain persistence
without the main container being modified.

**What to check:**
- Are mutating admission webhooks audited and restricted?
  ```
  kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations
  ```
- Can non-admin service accounts patch running pod specs?
  ```
  kubectl auth can-i patch pods --all-namespaces --as <service-account>
  ```
- Are all containers in pods validated against an image allow-list?

**Remediation:** Restrict `patch` and `update` on pods to only trusted service accounts
(e.g., Istio operator, approved service mesh). Audit all MutatingWebhookConfigurations —
each webhook should have a narrow `namespaceSelector` and be from a trusted source.
Use image admission policies (CODE-014) to ensure injected images come from approved registries.

items.title: Run SAST Scanning on Every Pull Request
items.description:
Static Application Security Testing (SAST) tools analyse source code for security
vulnerabilities without executing it. Running SAST on every PR as a blocking gate prevents
critical vulnerabilities from reaching the main branch.

**What to check:** Verify that a SAST tool (e.g., Semgrep, CodeQL, Snyk Code, Checkov
for IaC) is configured in the CI pipeline. Confirm that PR merges are blocked when
High or Critical severity findings are reported.

**Remediation:** Add a SAST step to the CI workflow (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins).
Configure branch protection rules to require the SAST check to pass. Triage and remediate
findings before merging.

references.title: NIST SP 800-218 — Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF)
OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K02: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities

items.title: Scan Dependencies for Known Vulnerabilities
items.description:
Third-party libraries and container base images frequently contain CVEs. Software
Composition Analysis (SCA) tools identify vulnerable dependencies before they are shipped
to production. The Log4Shell (Log4j) and XZ Utils incidents demonstrated the catastrophic
impact of unscanned supply chains.

**What to check:** Verify an SCA tool is wired into the CI pipeline (e.g., Trivy, Grype,
Snyk, Dependabot). Confirm High/Critical CVEs block deployment. Confirm base image scans
are included, not just application dependencies.

**Remediation:** Add `trivy fs .` or equivalent to the CI workflow. Enable Dependabot or
Renovate for automated dependency PRs. Set policy thresholds to block on CVSS ≥ 7.0 (High).

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K02: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities

items.title: Scan Repositories for Hardcoded Secrets
items.description:
Developers occasionally commit API keys, database passwords, and private keys directly
into source code. Once in git history, these credentials are extremely difficult to fully
remove and may already have been exfiltrated. Secret scanning tools detect these commits
before or immediately after they land.

**What to check:** Verify a secret scanning tool is active on all repositories:
- GitHub: GitHub Advanced Security / Secret Scanning alerts enabled
- GitLab: Secret Detection CI job enabled
- Self-hosted: GitGuardian, TruffleHog, or gitleaks wired into CI

Confirm pre-commit hooks are deployed to developer workstations to catch secrets before push.

**Remediation:** Enable native secret scanning in the SCM. Add a pre-commit hook with
`gitleaks` or `detect-secrets`. Rotate any detected credentials immediately. Invalidate
old git history with `git-filter-repo` if necessary.

items.title: Scan IaC Files for Security Misconfigurations
items.description:
Terraform, Helm charts, and Kubernetes YAML manifests are code that defines infrastructure
and workload configuration. Misconfigurations in these files — such as open security groups,
missing encryption, or permissive RBAC — are common root causes of cloud breaches.

**What to check:** Verify an IaC scanner is integrated into CI:
- Terraform: `tfsec`, `checkov`, or `terrascan`
- Helm charts: `helm lint` + `trivy config` or `polaris`
- YAML manifests: `kube-score`, `kubeconform`, `checkov`

**Remediation:** Add a scanning step to CI that fails on High/Critical findings. Add
pre-commit hooks for local feedback. Integrate with PR comments for developer visibility.

references.title: OWASP Kubernetes Top 10 — K01: Insecure Workload Configurations
ISO 27001 A.14 — System Acquisition and Development

items.title: Sign Container Images and Verify at Admission
items.description:
Container image signing creates a cryptographic attestation that an image was built by a
trusted pipeline and has not been tampered with. Combined with an admission webhook that
validates signatures, this prevents unsigned or compromised images from running in the cluster.

**What to check:**
1. Are images signed in CI? (Cosign + Sigstore / Notary v2)
2. Is an admission controller verifying signatures before pods are scheduled?
   - Policy controllers: Kyverno `verifyImages`, Connaisseur, Ratify

```
cosign verify --key cosign.pub <registry>/<image>:<tag>
```

**Remediation:** Integrate `cosign sign` into the CI pipeline after image push. Deploy a
verification admission policy that rejects unsigned images. Store signing keys in a KMS.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Compromised Image in Registry

items.title: Generate an SBOM for Every Container Image
items.description:
A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is a machine-readable inventory of all components in a
container image — OS packages, language libraries, and their versions. SBOMs enable rapid
triage when a new CVE is disclosed (e.g., Log4Shell): instead of scanning all images
reactively, you query the SBOM index for affected packages in seconds.

**What to check:** Verify that SBOM generation is part of the CI/CD pipeline and that
SBOMs are stored alongside image artifacts (e.g., as OCI attestations or in an artifact
registry):

```
syft <image> -o spdx-json > sbom.json
cosign attest --predicate sbom.json --type spdxjson <image>
```

**Remediation:** Add Syft or Trivy SBOM generation to the CI workflow. Attach the SBOM as
an OCI attestation. Integrate with a vulnerability management platform that indexes SBOMs.

references.title: US Executive Order 14028 — Software Supply Chain Security

items.title: Use Private Container Registries with Authentication
items.description:
Pulling container images from public registries without authentication or storing images in
public repositories exposes workloads to image substitution attacks and leaks proprietary
code. All production images must be stored in private registries with access controls.

**What to check:**
- Are all image references pointing to a private registry (not `docker.io` public)?
- Are registry credentials stored as Kubernetes image pull secrets, not hardcoded?
- Is anonymous pull disabled on the registry?

```
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq '.items[].spec.containers[].image' | grep -v '<your-private-registry>'
```

**Remediation:** Migrate images to a private registry (ECR, GCR, ACR, Harbor). Create
`imagePullSecrets` referencing registry credentials. Disable anonymous pull in registry
settings. Scan images on push.

references.title: Kubernetes Docs — Pull an Image from a Private Registry
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Compromised Image in Registry
Microsoft Threat Matrix — Images from a Private Registry

items.title: Implement Retry Logic for Dependent Services
items.description:
Kubernetes starts containers in any order. A pod may be scheduled before its dependencies
(database, message broker, downstream API) are ready. Applications that crash immediately
on `ECONNREFUSED` or similar startup errors create fragile deployment sequences that
require manual restarts or complex `initContainers` workarounds.

Instead, the application should retry the dependency connection with exponential backoff
until it succeeds or a configurable deadline is exceeded. This applies to both initial
startup connections and transient mid-runtime failures.

**What to check:**
- Does the app crash (non-zero exit) immediately when a dependency is unreachable at startup?
- Is there a retry loop with configurable max attempts and backoff for database/service connections?
- Does the application recover automatically if a dependency becomes temporarily unavailable?

**Remediation:** Wrap dependency initialisation in a retry loop with exponential backoff
and jitter (start at 1s, double up to 30s). Suitable libraries:
- Python: `tenacity`
- JVM: `resilience4j`, `spring-retry`
- Go: `github.com/cenkalti/backoff`
- Node.js: `async-retry`

Do not rely solely on `initContainers` for this — they only handle startup order, not
mid-runtime reconnection.

items.title: Log to stdout and stderr
items.description: Applications should perform passive logging: they should print log output directly to `stdout` and `stderr` instead of making network connections to intermediate aggregators or writing to database/index sinks directly. This allows external tools or daemonsets to collect, format, and route the logs cleanly.

items.title: Externalise Application Configuration
items.description: Configuration logic should be abstracted away from the application code itself to facilitate updates without recompilation. Hardcoded settings inhibit portability. Supply environment-specific configurations via ConfigMaps, and pass them down as either mounted files or environment variables.

items.title: Allow Deploying Containers Only from Known Registries
items.description:
Prevent arbitrary code execution by restricting the container registries from which Kubernetes can pull images. 
Use a custom policy engine (like Open Policy Agent or Kyverno) to enforce that workloads only run images sourced 
from approved, internal, or trusted external registries.

references.title: Microsoft Threat Matrix — Compromised Image in Registry

items.title: Enforce Uniqueness in Ingress Hostnames
items.description:
Without proper validation, a user might create an Ingress manifest that reuses an existing hostname, potentially 
hijacking or overriding traffic for an established service. Implement a validation webhook (e.g., via OPA) to 
check existing Ingress resources and deny the creation of duplicate hostnames across the cluster.

references.title: Open Policy Agent — Kubernetes Ingress Tutorial

items.title: Use Only Approved Domain Names in Ingress Hostnames
items.description:
Block the usage of arbitrary or unauthorized domain names in Ingress manifests. Implement a custom admission 
policy that rejects Ingress resources attempting to route traffic through invalid or unapproved hostnames, 
ensuring all external routing aligns with corporate DNS policies.

references.title: Open Policy Agent — Kubernetes Ingress Tutorial
